description: A 'muneki' is a place or fixture arranged to receive bodily waste. It covers pit latrines, composting arrangements, and water-carried fixtures without treating one design as the standard. The word identifies the daily place; treatment and sanitation method remain parts of its actual system.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for voiced 'm', carry the sound through the nose, and round into high-back 'u'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n', keep nasal airflow, and hold stressed true-mid front 'e'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into high-front 'i'.
sound_symbolism: Closed lips give 'mu' a low nasal chamber. Stressed 'ne' moves the contact to the teeth before final 'ki' closes farther back and rises into a bright vowel. The form climbs through three increasingly crisp points of contact.
usage_notes: Latrines are countable. Location and access can be described with the water use. Maintenance and the treatment path belong to the same account. Exact health instructions and technical classifications remain with their sources.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A latrine places water, nutrients, and pathogens inside an ordinary household system. Labour and access shape how that system is used and maintained.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Pit placement and dry cover carried practical knowledge about keeping bodily waste away from drinking water. Regular cleaning carried the rest.

semantic_domains.physical: a fixture or prepared place that receives bodily waste
semantic_domains.spatial: a bounded sanitation place in or near a dwelling
